title: Pope Leo XIV makes AI the subject of his first encyclical
subtitle: Magnifica Humanitas calls for robust regulation of artificial intelligence and warns that every design choice reflects a vision of humanity.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: Pope Leo XIV released his first encyclical on Monday, May 25, 2026, and made artificial intelligence its central subject. Titled Magnifica Humanitas — 'Magnificent Humanity' — the roughly 42,000-word document is subtitled 'on safeguarding the human person in the time of artificial intelligence.' Leo signed it on May 15, exactly 135 years after Pope Leo XIII signed Rerum Novarum, the 1891 text that founded modern Catholic social teaching in response to the Industrial Revolution. The choice of date was deliberate: Leo XIV is framing AI as the defining transformation of this era the way industrial labor was of the last. He calls for robust state and international regulation of AI, and appeals to 'disarm' the technology by separating it from military and economic interests.

An encyclical is the most authoritative form of papal teaching, so the topic itself is the signal. The Catholic Church has roughly 1.4 billion members, and a pope rarely spends his first encyclical — the document that sets the tone for a pontificate — on a single technology. The argument is not anti-technology. Its core claim is that 'technology is never neutral, because it takes on the characteristics of those who devise, finance, regulate and use it,' and that AI developers therefore 'bear a particular ethical and spiritual responsibility, for every design choice reflects a vision of humanity.' Leo invokes the principle of subsidiarity — that decisions should be made at the most local level possible — to criticize the concentration of power in a small technocratic class.

The encyclical lands in a year when AI governance has stalled in much of the world. The United States shelved a federal AI executive order in May, the EU spent the spring simplifying rather than tightening its AI Act, and the largest labs now command valuations and compute budgets that rival nation-states. Leo's intervention places a moral frame on questions that have mostly been argued in economic and technical terms: inequality, the erosion of democracy, the value of human work, and the meaning of human dignity. It situates AI inside the same tradition that runs from Rerum Novarum through Centesimus Annus and Laudato Si', the 2015 encyclical on the environment.

Takeaway for learners: the line worth carrying out of this document is that 'every design choice reflects a vision of humanity.' Whatever your beliefs, that is a useful engineering principle. When you set a default, choose a metric to optimize, or decide what a system will and will not do, you are encoding a view of what matters and who counts. The most employable people in AI over the next decade will not only be those who can build — they will be those who can explain the human consequences of a design decision to a regulator, an executive, or a customer who is asking exactly the question the Pope just put on the world's front pages.
